title: Prayer in the Aftermath of the San Francisco Earthquake, by Rabbi Bernard M. Kaplan (19 May 1906)
author: Aharon N. Varady (transcription)

coauthors.name: Aharon N. Varady (transcription)
coauthors.bio: Aharon Varady (M.A.J.Ed./JTSA Davidson) is a volunteer transcriber for the Open Siddur Project. If you find any mistakes in his transcriptions, please let him know. Shgiyot mi yavin; Ministarot naqeni שְׁגִיאוֹת מִי־יָבִין; מִנִּסְתָּרוֹת נַקֵּנִי "Who can know all one's flaws? From hidden errors, correct me" (Psalms 19:13). If you'd like to directly support his work, please consider donating via his Patreon account. (Varady also translates prayers and contributes his own original work besides serving as the primary shammes of the Open Siddur Project and its website, opensiddur.org.)

coauthors.name: Bernard Kaplan
coauthors.bio: Rabbi Bernard M. Kaplan was an early Conservative movement rabbi in Canada and the United States. After his ordination from the Jewish Theological Seminary in New York, he was received with acclaim at Shaar Hashomayim in Montreal in 1897. He also served as principal of the synagogue's day school and as Dominion Hebrew chaplain of Montreal's prison system. In 1902, he left for California and Bush Street Temple in San Francisco. If you know more about the life and career of Rabbi Kaplan, please contact us.

excerpt: This prayer by Rabbi Bernard Kaplan in the aftermath of the San Francisco earthquake of 1906 was published in the Jewish newspaper, Emanu-El (v. 22, n. 2, 18 May 1906), p. 8, under the title "Rabbi Kaplan's Prayer."
content:
The following special prayer was delivered by Rabbi Bernard M. Kaplan at the Bush Street Temple, San Francisco, at the Sabbath service after the awful earthquake of April 18, 1906:

Contribute a translation Source (English)
Almighty God,
Source of all power, justice, life and love!
We recognize Thy love and mercy
in times of sorrow and sadness
as well as in joy and gladness,
in times of calamity and disaster
as well as in times of prosperity.
We resign ourselves to Thy inscrutable decree.
As true children love their father
though when punished and rebuked,
so do we love Thee and adore Thy holy name
though we have been sorely, sorely afflicted
by this most appalling visitation of the earthquake and fire.
We believe, O God,
that as a father punishes his children for their own good,
so Thy punishment is meant for good and not for evil,
for Thou art all good and all merciful.
We thank Thee, O Lord,
that this calamity
which has befallen our beautiful city
and our beloved community
has not occurred during the dark hours of the night
nor during the working hours of the day,
when the destruction of life would have been very great,
and when horror would have been added to horror
and gloom to gloom.
Yea, in all Thy acts and decrees
we perceive Thy love and Thy mercy,
which increases our faith in Thee
and in Thy everlasting goodness.
We thank Thee, O Lord,
for the brave spirit with which the people of San Francisco
are standing this most awful trial and visitation.
We thank Thee, O Lord,
for the great humanity of the people of this community
who in moments of the greatest danger
have endeavored to render help
to their fellow sufferers,
even at the risk of their lives.
We thank Thee, O Lord,
for the great generosity of the American people
who are coming to the rescue
of our afflicted and stricken community.
Inspire, O Heavenly Father,
the people of San Francisco
with new courage and hope,
and with renewed faith in themselves
and in Thee,
so that they may help this city
to rise Phoenix-like
still more beautiful,
still more glorious,
and still more hospitable and generous
than ever before,
for the glory of Thy name
and for the benefit of humanity.
Amen.

This prayer by Rabbi Bernard Kaplan in the aftermath of the San Francisco earthquake of 1906 was published in the Jewish newspaper, Emanu-El (v. 22, n. 2, 18 May 1906), p. 8, under the title “Rabbi Kaplan’s Prayer.”

Source(s)

Rabbi Kaplan’s Prayer (Bernard M. Kaplan in Emanu-El, 18 May 1906), p. 8
